data.title: EM Bonds Shine As DM Bonds Struggle With Fiscal Drift
data.description:
EM bonds continue to outperform on fiscal discipline and CNY strength, while indebted DMs struggle with weak policy and unpopular governments.

The VanEck Emerging Markets Bond ETF† was up 1.70% in September, compared to 1.59% for its benchmark, the 50% J.P. Morgan Government Bond Index - Emerging Markets Global Diversified (GBI-EM) and 50% J.P. Morgan Emerging Markets Bond Index (EMBI). Year to date, the ETF is up 15.41%, compared to 13.04% for its benchmark, and 8.12% and 6.96% for the Global Agg and 10-year Treasuries, respectively. Argentina was a big mover this month (and an outperformer for the ETF). We touch on it inside, though we caution that there is a significant global oversupply of opinions on Argentina. Here’s ours: we didn’t like Argentina because policy was way ahead of politics and there was a consensus overweight/bullish view in markets and among cab drivers globally. Then politics arose, bonds puked, and there was a chance of a fairly fast and easy bridge loan from the US (which then happened). So, when it crashed, we got bullish. It’s a trade, we are still in a heavily game-theoretic stage. We remain very bullish on local currency, while somewhat cautious on USD duration especially with low spreads. The ETF has around 55.3% in curated local currency, the rest in mostly higher-yielding USD bonds. Carry is 6.4%, yield to worst (YTW) is 8.1%, and duration is 5.3. The maximum for local is 60% and our bias remains to be close to the maximum.

Average Annual Total Returns* (%) (In USD)

As of September 30, 2025
1 Mo 3 Mo YTD 1 Yr 3 Yrs 5 Yrs 10 Yrs
EMBX (NAV) 1.70 4.40 15.41 9.72 13.39 4.87 5.27
EMBX (Share Price) 1.70 4.40 15.41 9.72 13.39 4.87 5.27
50% GBI-EM/50% EMBI 1.59 3.78 13.05 7.98 11.82 2.34 3.93

* Returns less than one year are not annualized.

Expenses: Class A: Gross 1.83%, Net 1.21%; Class I: Gross 1.37%, Net 0.86%; Class Y: Gross 1.33%, Net 0.96%. Expenses are capped contractually until 5/01/26 at 1.20% for Class A, 0.85% for Class I, 0.95% for Class Y. Caps exclude acquired fund fees and expenses, interest, trading, dividends, and interest payments of securities sold short, taxes, and extraordinary expenses.

The performance data quoted represents past performance. Past performance is not a guarantee of future results. Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost.

Performance may be lower or higher than performance data quoted. Please call 800.826.2333 or visit vaneck.com for performance current to the most recent month ended.

The “Net Asset Value” (NAV) of a Fund is determined at the close of each business day , and represents the dollar value of one share of the fund; it is calculated by taking the total asset of the fund, subtracting total liabilities, and dividing by the total number of shares outstanding. The NAV is not necessarily the same of the ETF’s intraday trading value. Investors should not expect to buy or sell shares at NAV.

Prior to 10/06/2025, the Fund operated as the VanEck Emerging Markets Bond mutual fund; performance shown before that date is that fund’s NAV performance (Class I, unadjusted for today’s ETF expenses).

The big wheel turning remains “fiscal dominance,” in which low-indebted EMs with inflation-focused central banks win, and high-indebted DMs with arguably co-opted central banks lose. We got reminders of the bad – UK financing worries, a EU that looks not to be seizing the moment to issue mutualized debt when the market wants it, a new PM in France with immediate protests over fiscal policy…sigh. EM gave us more reminders of the good. Mexico’s first budget under newly elected President Sheinbaum met market expectations of orthodoxy. Indonesia re-committed to its fiscal targets after a bout of political turmoil that saw its finance minister leave. (Now, on Indonesia, we must say that we have low confidence in this commitment, but our point is that the government rushed to reassure on orthodoxy; whether they are serious is a separate question.) South Africa is arguably in a goldilocks situation, with its always-excellent monetary policy now complemented by improved fiscal outcomes. China is chugging along smoothly, with the market hopefully realizing that weak growth outcomes will be met with fiscal stimulus (which will be reduced if strong growth outcomes materialize). And we have deepening reform in Ecuador under its IMF program, and surprising positive policy tilts in Zambia, just to mention some of the off-the-beaten-path names.

“Fiscal dominance” also happens to map to social and political risk. A classic outcome is higher inflation, which reduces the real value of debt at the expense of social and political peace, as well as attempts to downplay inflation in nominal GDP narratives. A cursory look at DM headlines tells you we are at this stage. EM politics, on the other hand, are generally characterized by strength, popularity, and economic orthodoxy. Just look at Exhibit 1, which shows government popularity (defined as “approval” and using data sources chosen by Perplexity). We always like to remind investors that it is hard (and getting harder) to find relevant political parties in EMs that can get away with heterodoxy – voters aren’t as susceptible to promises of fiscal profligacy or harnessing central banks to gin up growth. Mexico’s supposedly left-wing government is economically orthodox and generated higher real incomes for Mexicans, for example – that is a typical and arguably growing formula inside EMs. Roughly half of consumption in many of our countries is food and energy, so voters have disciplined politicians over decades.

Exhibit 1 – EMs Have Popular (and Orthodox) Governments, DMs Have Unpopular and Flailing Governments

Government Popularity

If “fiscal dominance” is the big wheel, CNY remains the driving wheel where actual asset-price stuff starts happening…and it keeps strengthening steadily. This remains one of the most important dynamics for all markets and remains incredibly overlooked. EMs trade more with China than with the US, so this is a huge tailwind to EM currencies (see Exhibit 2). And remember the start of the year or even last month? The consensus view was that tariffs would destroy EM currencies. The opposite has happened. We’ve written extensively on this, including our piece “The Curiously Unpopular Case for RMB/CNY Appreciation”. Now, as we said in the piece, CNY itself is not the way to express this view. It will be tightly managed, and the appreciation will be like watching paint dry. Lucky for us, we can invest in bonds/currencies that are allowed to move more freely; see below for the big names. We should also point out that the ETF has no exposure to India local currency, which performed poorly in 2025, as we see it as broadly “not ready for primetime”. We are also cautious/underweight on Indonesia local, also a loser this year.

Exhibit 2 – EM Currencies Keep Rallying; Every Possible Cause Cited Other Than CNY

A look at Argentina. The ratio of the number of opinions on Argentina relative to its importance is astronomical. For decades this has been the case. From cab drivers to heads of state – I’m always getting asked about trivial Argentina and never about Indonesia, Brazil, South Africa, or Nigeria. It’s incredible. Our opinion is actually pretty simple, and we’re also intimating that the world doesn’t need more opinions on Argentina…but here’s ours.

We didn’t like Argentina. Policy was way ahead of politics. Then it blew up in September and bond prices crashed. And, there was a chance of a fairly fast and easy bridge loan from the US. And, the market was consensus overweight, even the cab drivers. So, when it crashed, we got bullish. It’s a trade; we are still in a heavily game-theoretic stage. But, the government can probably get through to midterm elections with protecting reserves and actually buying back short-dated USD debt (which we now own). We’ll see what we think at that next decision node, the midterm elections.

Exposure Types and Significant Changes

The changes to our top positions are summarized below. Our largest positions in September were South Africa, Mexico, Poland, Thailand and Malaysia:

  • We increased our local currency exposure in South Africa and Poland. South Africa’s “sleeper” fiscal story is not fully appreciated by the market, but the country’s fiscal performance continues to improve, and the latest move – considering a formal fiscal rule – would be a major structural breakthrough. In terms of our investment process, this has strengthened South Africa’s policy test score. Poland’s fiscal situation looks more concerning, but this is unlikely to affect net bond issuance and slow fiscal consolidation (which in part reflects less inflationary defense spending) is mostly priced in. Poland’s central bank remains credible, and the currency is a proxy for the Europe’s revival theme, which improves the technical test score for the country.
  • We also increased our hard currency sovereign exposure in Argentina and the Republic of Congo. The level of political noise in Argentina will remain elevated in the run up to the mid-term elections, but the U.S. decision to provide a large-scale support package (which came after a major selloff) is likely to be a game-changer for the post-election period, strengthening the policy test score for the country. Congo’s multi-billion dollar deal with China to boost oil production had a positive impact on the country’s economic and policy test scores.
  • Finally, we increased our local currency exposure in Mexico and Chile. Mexico is a major winner in Trade War 2.0, with supportive domestic backdrop (gradual fiscal consolidation, disinflation, and credible rate cuts being the most relevant for local bonds). The global commodity/copper price backdrop remains beneficial for the Chilean currency, the central bank might deliver another rate cut, and the presidential election outcome is not expected to derail Chile’s orthodox policy framework. In terms of our investment process, this improves the technical and policy test scores for the country.
  • We reduced our local currency exposure in Indonesia on the back of the constant flow of negative fiscal news, which reflect the government’s increasingly pro-growth policy stance. An additional structural complication is an attempt to expand the central bank’s mandate. Both factors worsened the country’s policy test score. Further, Indonesia’s economy will be negatively affected by the Grasberg mine incident – it lowered the economic test score for the country.
  • We also reduced our local currency exposure and hard currency corporate exposure in Brazil. The latter reflected corporate fraud allegations. Regarding local bonds, we took partial profits after the huge year-to-date rally, which dented the technical test score for the country. We are likely to take it back up.
  • Finally, we reduced our local currency exposure in China and hard currency sovereign exposure in Bosnia and Herzegovina. Bosnia and Herzegovina is making structural progress to access the EU funds (Growth Plan for the Western Balkans), but valuations are less attractive now, so we decided to employ the funds in more compelling opportunities. We also took partial profits in China, since the anti-involution policy push is expected to lead to higher domestic prices, and hence, higher local yields, worsening the policy test score for the country.

Disclosures

†On October 6th, 2025, the Fund converted from an open‑end mutual fund into an exchange‑traded fund (“ETF”). As part of the conversion, the Fund adopted the accounting and performance history of its predecessor mutual fund (the “Predecessor Fund”). Effective October 6th, 2025, the Fund’s total annual operating expenses changed; returns for periods prior to that date reflect the Predecessor Fund’s expenses then in effect.

Duration measures a bond's sensitivity to interest rate changes that reflects the change in a bond's price given a change in yield. This duration measure is appropriate for bonds with embedded options. Carry is the benefit or cost for owning an asset. Yield to worst is a measure of the lowest possible yield that can be received on a bond with an early retirement provision. Averages are market weighted. The yields presented do not represent the performance of the Fund. These statistics do not take into account fees and expenses associated with investments of the Fund.

All indices are unmanaged and include the reinvestment of all dividends, but do not reflect the payment of transaction costs, advisory fees or expenses that are associated with an investment in the Fund. Certain indices may take into account withholding taxes. An index's performance is not illustrative of the Fund's performance. Indices are not securities in which investments can be made.

The Fund's benchmark index (50% GBI-EM/50% EMBI) is a blended index consisting of 50% J.P. Morgan Government Bond Index-Emerging Markets (GBI-EM) Global Diversified and 50% J.P. Morgan Emerging Markets Bond Index (EMBI). The J.P. Morgan GBI-EM Global Diversified tracks local currency bonds issued by Emerging Markets governments. The J.P. Morgan EMBI Global Diversified tracks returns for actively traded external debt instruments in emerging markets, and is also J.P. Morgan's most liquid U.S. dollar emerging markets debt benchmark.

The Bloomberg Global Aggregate Index measures the performance of global investment grade fixed income securities.

The FTSE Treasury Benchmark 10-year measures the return of the 10-year U.S. Treasury.

ICE BofA Global Broad Market Index tracks the performance of investment grade debt publicly issued in the major domestic and eurobond markets, including sovereign, quasi-government, corporate, securitized and collateralized securities. ICE BofA Current 10-Year U.S. Treasury Index is comprised of the most recently issued 10-year U.S. Treasury note.

The MSCI ACWI Index is a global equity benchmark that captures large- and mid-cap stocks across 23 developed and 24 emerging markets, representing approximately 85% of the global investable equity universe.

The S&P 500 Index is a widely recognized U.S. equity benchmark that tracks 500 of the largest publicly traded companies, reflecting the performance of the core U.S. stock market.

Information has been obtained from sources believed to be reliable but J.P. Morgan does not warrant its completeness or accuracy. The Index is used with permission. data.title: Dell: Re-Rating In Process, More Upside Likely, Raising My Price Target
data.description:
The Information Technology sector has been the clear standout area of the S&P 500 since the market bottom six months ago. The Information Technology Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLK) is actually the lone outperforming sector fund since April 9. Throw a dart, and you’ll likely land on a winner in the space.

In May, I had a "Buy" rating on Dell Technologies (NYSE:DELL), a so-called “legacy” tech name, but it has been in the process of re-rating. I think there’s still room for it to run. Ahead of earnings in November, I reiterate a "Buy" rating.

Shares are up 76% since my previous analysis, outperforming the S&P 500 by more than 55 percentage points. I’m lifting my price target, though not incredibly so, and will note a key spot on the chart to monitor.

XLK: The Only Leading Sector Since the April Low

Back in August, Dell reported a solid set of quarterly results. Q2 non-GAAP EPS of $2.32 topped the Wall Street consensus target of $2.29, while revenue of $29.8 billion, up 19% from the same period a year earlier, was a significant $610 million beat. The company issued bullish guidance, seeing full-year sales between $105 billion and $109 billion and earnings rising 25% year-on-year.

Shares plunged by 8.9% in the session that followed, however, a fourth consecutive decline post-earnings. Ahead of the November 25 Q3 2026 report, the options market prices in a high 8.7% earnings-related stock price swing when analyzing the at-the-money straddle expiring soonest after the reporting date.

Looking back on the quarter that was, Dell asserted itself as a top player in the ever-growing AI hardware market. Amid booming demand for AI servers, the management team has successfully pivoted to new demand spots, and we see that in the numbers. Strong revenue growth, high free cash flow, and soaring EPS indicate Dell’s position. In Q2, AI server sales were $8.2 billion—above previous guidance of just $7 billion. That was likely the impetus for the firm to lift its full-year AI server revenue target by $5 billion to $20 billion for the current year.

Margins will, of course, be in focus in its Q3 report, along with demand from enterprise and sovereign. Its overall Infrastructure Solutions Group (ISG) saw its top line climb by 44%, helping to deliver $2.5 billion in operating cash flow. With record-breaking top and bottom lines in Q2, the bar may be high for the Q3 report. Dell sees second-half EPS growth of 17%, helped by firm margins. Just this week, shares rallied after bullish comments at its Investor Day.

On the earnings outlook, analysts see strong EPS growth in FY 2026 and throughout the year. FY 2028 could see a slight deceleration in the bottom line, but the change is not much different from the pace of earnings growth over the upcoming quarters. Sales growth should ease from the low double-digits to the high single-digits in the next 12 months.

The sell-side, meanwhile, is very upbeat. There have been 18 EPS upgrades over the past 90 days, compared to just a pair of downgrades. Finally, on profitability, Dell’s free cash flow per share is now up to $7.01 over the past four quarters (it was just $2.64 in May).

Dell: Revenue & Earnings Outlooks, EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, I have key updates. I previously set a value at $154. I’m raising it to $179. Here’s how I got there.

If we assume normalized forward operating EPS of $10.50, near the current consensus, and apply a 17x multiple (still at a discount to the sector and overall market), then we arrive at $179. I’d also note that 17x is below Dell’s long-term average.

Shares also look attractive on a price-to-sales basis.

Dell: Still Compelling on Earnings and Sales

Key risks for Dell include a decline in its storage revenue, as its customers face many options there. Also, the company’s AI-related margins are not all that high (just 2%-2.5%), so if the margin dips, that could translate into weaker EPS growth.

Bigger picture, a macro slowdown or some kind of bubble burst in the AI theme would certainly result in lower earnings projections and likely a reduced multiple. Trade war escalations with China could also put Dell in a bad light.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show a confirmed Q3 2026 earnings date of Tuesday, Nov. 25 AMC. Shares trade ex a $0.525 dividend on Tuesday, Oct. 21. The company also presents at a pair of industry conferences before the reporting date.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With shares still undervalued in my view, Dell’s technical situation is solid. Notice in the chart below that the stock broke out from a broad declining pattern just a few weeks ago. The stock has been a laggard, and I questioned my bullish thesis for a while after the May write-up. But with the high-volume ascent, we see that the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph confirmed the price advance, while the long-term 200-day moving average is turning up (also a strong sign).

Also take a look at the volume by price profile on the left side of the chart—there's now a high amount of shares traded below today’s level. That should offer support if we see a pullback. As it stands, support is seen in the low $140s, which is where the rising 50dma should enter the scene soon. Resistance is at the 2024 high of $180.

DELL: $180 Resistance, $140 Support, Rising 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a "Buy" rating on DELL. I see the Information Technology stock as still attractive on valuation. Long-term, I would not be surprised to see shares be re-rated closer to a market multiple. For now, I see additional upside into earnings and year-end.

data.title: Why A Bad Deal Makes AMD A Strong Buy
data.description:
AMD (NASDAQ:AMD) recently announced a major commitment by OpenAI to buy a truckload of its Instinct GPUs. The economics of the deal are somewhat unclear as of now. I’ll work through them as well as I can. OpenAI basically wants six gigawatts of machines starting with the 450 generation delivered in the 2nd half of 2026 and then spread out over a number of years. According to AMD this adds up to double digit billion of incremental revenue from 2026 and “tens of billions” (so let’s assume at least $20 billion) of annual revenue once deliveries are fully ramped up (2027). An important catch is that AMD gave out 160 million warrants to incentivize OpenAI to go for this deal. This makes it really hard to work out what the deal really means. Before I get into that, let's put the deal into context:

Here are analysts current revenue estimates for AMD:

Seeking Alpha data shows analysts estimated $41 billion in annual revenue for 2026 and $53 billion for 2027. These targets should get blown out of the water with this new deal.

Last quarter AMD did only $3.2 billion in data center revenue, and its growth rate was only 14% (well under the industry growth rate / driven by CPUs) and it wasn't even profitable on the segment.

AMD is emphatic that the deal is incremental to GAAP earnings, and the CFO emphasized huge operating leverage from the boost in revenue.

Just this deal is in the ballpark of 166% data center growth in the first year. It is a ~$100 billion deal (spread out over multiple years) in a segment where AMD is doing ~$12 billion at last quarter's run-rate on an annual basis. If we assume AMD makes $20 billion in annual incremental revenue per year of this deal, it adds around $5 billion per quarter. That adds up to data center growth of 166%.

I recently wrote an article Breaking The Cycle: How AI Transforms Memory Stock Micron and there I wrote:

AMD has a plan to try and grab a little bit of GPU marketshare. The main hurdle isn't hardware specs, but its software ecosystem, which is pretty far behind CUDA. AMD's plan deserves its own article. I'll write about it in the near future. However, if AMD manages to gain a modest share, I expect it could be a small positive for memory demand. AMD uses a chiplet design, which makes it a little bit easier to include a lot of memory on its GPU's.

It is a bit unfortunate I didn't finish the article about AMD's plan, which is to capture GPU share among the largest customers by having narrowed the software gap around mid-2026 with a competitive product on training and inference.

I'm a bit disappointed AMD issued so many warrants to close the deal. The way I understand GAAP accountancy (note that I'm not an accountant), AMD will have to book the warrants as a discount or a refund on revenue. It probably needs to determine a fair value of the warrants at the time it issues them (not when they vest!).

They’ll likely get valued with the Black Scholes Model. There is currently so little information available about these warrants it is hard to say what fair value will look like. My guess is that "fair value" gets set at between $5 billion and $12 billion based on the value of long-dated AMD call options.

Black Scholes assumes there’s not really a direction to the options valued and doesn’t reflect that this is a case where the options are issued to a party with significant agency (like buying more AMD or developing AMD's open-source software) to determine whether they’ll end up in the money or not.

However, if the 160 million warrants ultimately end up as shares, the package is worth around $33 billion at current prices. However, they only vest at certain out-of-the-money levels. AMD disclosed the last tranche only vests with AMD at $600. If AMD reaches $600 and OpenAI holds on to the shares, the package should be worth at least around $90 billion. If you look at it that way, OpenAI may have almost fully recouped its investment.

The advantage to AMD is it is able to give out a significant discount and incentivize OpenAI to buy a lot more of its products. Meanwhile, AMD helps its margins because revenue is optically reduced while the earnings are not directly affected. Because GAAP accounting says the warrants have to be booked at a discount to the revenue. It will look like a few billion dollars less of revenue is coming in. This is actually favorable to your margin profile. It is probably not favorable in terms of how you get taxed (note that I'm not a tax expert either).

If the cost of the warrant was subtracted from profits instead of revenue (which I would argue is closer to economic reality), margins on the deal could go to zero. That's still accretive compared to last quarter's data center margins but not to the overall business. I'd think this would save some taxes. Now AMD risks ending up paying taxes on profits that don't really exist except in accountancy.

There is no way around the ultimate economic impact of giving away warrants. Once they get exercised, earnings are diluted across a larger number of shares, and EPS decreases. This warrant structure is likely part of the reason why AMD could so emphatically argue the deal is hugely accretive to GAAP earnings.

At the end of the day, the economics aren’t as bad as giving away 10% of your market cap (which comments on X would suggest), but there’s significant value to these warrants, and both the Black Scholes fair value models and the accountancy treatment will camouflage the true value of this discount in my view.

Bears will argue AMD is giving its products away, and OpenAI can't pay for anything (as it doesn't have the cash or free cash flow) to pay for this. I think it is possible to be overly skeptical. Sure, the deal could fall through. However, OpenAI is growing revenue very rapidly. It isn't profitable exactly because it is buying compute by the boatload. There's a risk OpenAI can't get enough capital or revenue together to live up to the deals for compute. Currently, the risk looks relatively small (the warrant deal mitigates the risk further), but it does exist.

I get why a lot of people get worked up about these kinds of circular deals that also make it really hard to read what’s really going on with the financials. At the same time, there is a reasonable rationale for it from both sides. As bad as it looks at first glance (a lot depending on the specifics of the warrants), it is a meaningful step forward for AMD.

At a recent Goldman Sachs Conference AMD said it wanted to start competing more seriously with Nvidia at the 450 generation, which should go up against Nvidia’s Vera Rubin. A key problem for AMD is that it is a few steps behind in terms of software. AMD has been working very hard to narrow this gap. Specifically to try and level the playing field for the 20% largest customers.

For most developers, AMD will continue to be meaningfully behind for the foreseeable future, but they may have a shot with the big ones, as exemplified by this deal. I wouldn't be surprised if Oracle (ORCL) signs a deal with AMD next.

AMD’s software is open-source as opposed to the proprietary CUDA. This is a major reason why it may be worth it for AMD to give out some incentives for major customers to switch over. These will start expanding the open-source software system.

All the hyperscalers are interested in having AMD in the game to put a little bit of pressure on Nvidia's margins. There are many instances where a dominant proprietary software platform was ultimately overtaken by open source alternatives. At the same time, with the AI-race in full swing, no one wants to get bogged down significantly, not to mention incur a temporary cost disadvantage. The burden is on AMD to close the total cost difference with creative deals like this.

Conclusion

In my view, this isn’t necessarily a deal with great economics (optically it will look fine) for AMD in the short term. But the scaling law (for transformer models) still holds. Compute is still the obvious path for model builders to improve their product. Nvidia sports a $4.5 trillion market cap vs AMD’s $330 billion. AMD trades at around 50x earnings vs Nvidia at 40x earnings.

Getting such a serious commitment from one of the leading foundational model companies signals to the market that the open source platform ROCm is going to continue to grow, and AMD is bound to take this market (and the software to support it) very seriously.

It becomes much more likely other major customers are going to follow along. With each customer signing up, AMD's competitive position improves. AMD is a strong buy here as it offers asymmetric opportunity to the upside. If things don't work out at all, it could easily fall back to $100 or a bit below.

The obvious risk here is that OpenAI will fail to generate enough revenue fast enough or raise enough capital to honor its commitments. Three years ago OpenAI wasn't even on the radar. Now it is essentially a startup with a valuation well ahead of AMD itself. In most less favorable scenarios, I would expect AMD to retain a bit of a premium just because of the optionality of becoming competitive in the AI-market.

On the upside; a leading model builder is now heavily incentivized to see AMDs stock go up and reach $600 per share to see its warrants vest. Even with AMD trading well over the past few days that's still a 60% down vs 160% up proposition.

data.title: Biogen's Comeback: Why The Bulls Are Finally Winning Again
data.description:
Since my article "Biogen: Investors Are Missing the Bigger Picture," which provoked a sharp reaction primarily from investors and traders pessimistic about Biogen's long-term outlook (NASDAQ:BIIB), its stock price has risen 19%.

In it, I presented an analysis of clinical data on felzartamab in the treatment of lupus nephritis and immunoglobulin A nephropathy, Leqembi's prospects in the Alzheimer's therapeutics market, and explained why Biogen's balance sheet will continue to strengthen from 2025.

If I look at Biogen from a technical analysis perspective, after Wave 3 of the impulse completed on October 3, reaching the VWAP [$159.9], its share price corrected to a strong support level between $149.5 and $151.

I currently expect the 'bull run' to continue until the first standard deviation band formed from the VWAP [+1σ], namely $205.

However, before I discuss its financial results for Q2 2025 and what Wall Street expects from Biogen in Q3, I have a question.

Has Biogen made progress in developing its CNS portfolio beyond its Alzheimer's franchise?

In short, yes.

Zurzuvae continues to take over the postpartum depression treatment market

At the end of 2024, I thought Zurzuvae's commercial prospects were rather dim.

Why?

Biogen and its partner Sage Therapeutics [now part of Supernus] (SUPN) have decided not to continue developing this neuroactive steroid for the treatment of MDD [major depressive disorder], but will instead focus on a subtype called postpartum depression.

So, Barry Greene, CEO of Sage, stated the following on the Q3 2024 earnings call.

Now, as we are focused on PPD, Sage and Biogen will also not pursue further development of zuranolone as a treatment for major depressive disorder in the U.S. Based on the significant new investment and time we expect would be needed to conduct the additional studies, and we're prioritizing our resources on supporting the PPD patient community.

But my pessimism was shattered by the real numbers.

Zurzuvae sales reached $46.4 million for the three months ended June 30, 2025, up 211.4% year-over-year and 67.5% quarter-over-quarter.

I was also obliged to note that on September 17, Zurzuvae became the first medication to treat postpartum depression in the European Union, the symptoms of which occur in about 17% of women.

Surprised by the strong performance of Vumerity, Skyclarys and Leqembi

So, Biogen's revenue was about $2.65 billion in the second quarter of 2025, beating the consensus estimate by about $321.7 million, as well as my 'best-case scenario' by $145 million.

Here, I will clarify a little by saying that it increased by 7.7% year-on-year and 9.1% quarter-on-quarter despite a decline in Tecfidera sales, mainly due to competition from its generic versions produced by Viatris (VTRS), Amneal (AMRX), and Sandoz Group (OTCQX:SDZNY).

I also highlight Spinraza as a "dark spot" for Biogen.

Its sales were $392.7 million, down 8.5% from Q2 2024, in my opinion, due to neurologists increasingly choosing PTC Therapeutics/Roche Holding's Evrysdi for the treatment of SMA [spinal muscular atrophy] due to its ease of use [it's taken orally].

Meanwhile, Biogen's non-GAAP EPS "blew" my mind, coming in at $5.47. It beat my expectations by 74 cents, and more importantly, it increased by 81.1% quarter-on-quarter.

Why did I expect a more modest increase?

First, because Biogen is finally becoming more active in R&D, including launching a BRAVE Phase 3 clinical trial [NCT06953583] in mid-June to evaluate the efficacy of Skyclarys in treating children with Friedreich ataxia, and also signing a deal worth up to $1 billion with City Therapeutics to develop RNAi therapies.

And secondly, the "sales and marketing" associated with Leqembi, which I mentioned earlier, have continued to grow since its launch, namely, reaching $171.2 million in Q2 2025 compared to $137.7 million in Q2 2024.

This trend, which I've captured in the graph above, is understandable because Biogen and Eisai are actively working to more widely introduce this anti-Aβ monoclonal antibody into clinical practice, especially given that its competitor, Eli Lilly's Kisunla [donanemab] (LLY), is "hot on its heels".

Here I want to mention that on September 25, Eli Lilly announced that the European Commission had approved Kisunla for the treatment of certain people suffering from Alzheimer's disease.

Let's continue.

Which medications of Biogen contributed to its revenue and EBIT growth?

To answer this question, I noted the sales of its medications in a table, also dividing them into four categories, namely rare diseases, biosimilars, multiple sclerosis, and postpartum depression, which in turn includes only Zurzuvae.

What interesting and most significant things did I notice?

I believe the key point is that Biogen has made significant progress in developing the lecanemab franchise over the past five months.

More importantly, I'm seeing the weak confidence that prevailed, particularly among retail investors, regarding Leqembi's commercial prospects beginning to give way to cautious optimism.

Why?

Obviously, it's related to its sales.

However, let me be a little more precise here.

So, Leqembi global in-market sales reached $160 million in Q2 2025, increasing by 66.7% quarter-on-quarter and 4 times year-on-year.

This figure exceeded my "base case scenario" by $25 million since I factored into my model the more limited impact of Biogen's direct-to-consumer TV campaign, as well as the more modest demand for lecanemab in China.

Of the recent key milestones that, in my opinion, will contribute to the acceleration of Leqembi sales growth starting in Q3, I highlight the following.

First, its launch in the European Union, beginning with Austria [August 25, 2025] and Germany [September 1, 2025].

Second, on June 30, Biogen published promising data from a Phase 3 Clarity AD clinical trial in which this monoclonal antibody resulted in a notable decrease in cognitive decline, as measured by the CDR-SB scale [by 1.75 points after 4 years, compared to ADNI].

And its third achievement that caught my attention was the fact that on September 24, Australia's TGA finally approved Leqembi for the treatment of early Alzheimer's disease.

Risks

I've updated the table of risks associated with Biogen, ranging from pressure on the US pharmaceutical industry caused by Trump's statements to falling sales of Avonex [$177.7 million in Q2 2025 vs. $182.8 million in Q2 2024] and Tecfidera for the reasons I discussed earlier.

Takeaway

Finally, I want to mention the final milestone that I believe will boost lecanemab sales, namely the launch of its subcutaneous version called Leqembi Iqlik, as well as the "Leqembi Companion program" on October 6.

I believe this move by Biogen and Eisai will facilitate access to this antibody for the about 7.2 million Americans suffering from Alzheimer's disease.

On the other hand, thanks in part to Biogen raising its 2025 non-GAAP EPS guidance from $14.50-$15.50 to $15.50-$16.00, and also to the fact that Vumerity continues to offset the decline in Tecfidera sales, I believe its risk/reward profile remains attractive.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Resources Connection: Maintaining Hold Rating Although Q1 Earnings Encouraging
data.description:
Intro

Our most recent commentary on Resources Connection, Inc. (NASDAQ:RGP) was in May of this year, when we stated that the consulting-oriented business needed to begin reporting better earnings trends for us to upgrade the stock. Negative growth trends at the time, fueled by persistent impairment charges, continued to dampen investor sentiment, which is why we maintained our cautious stance (hold rating) in RGP. The one advantage, though (as we pointed out in May), is that the continuous fall in goodwill on the balance sheet would ultimately serve a purpose if investors were just patient enough. To this point, post the release of the company's first quarter earnings (released on 10/8/2025 after market close), shares are well up to pre-market (6%+ gains as I write, with investor sentiment turning bullish due to the convincing Q1 top- and bottom-line beats).

Q1 Earnings Report Demonstrated Strong Gross Margin Gains

RGP reported revenues of $120.23 million on GAAP earnings of -$0.07 per share. Although earnings did come in negative for the quarter, gross margin in particular (39.5%) enabled GAAP earnings to beat consensus estimates by a significant $0.10 per share. Gross margin trends drive profitability, demonstrating that slowly but surely, RGP's operating model is stabilizing, where better value propositions are being provided in a disciplined cost-management environment. This is evident through better average bill rates, more effective management of bench consultants, and a meaningful cut in employee-oriented expenses.

SG&A Trends Improving

Now, SG&A makes up the lion's share of RGP's gross profit ($47.49 million in Q1), but here also we are seeing sustained improvements. SG&A of $44.5 million came in $3.2 million lighter in the first quarter (7% improvement) compared to the same period a year prior, with further gains expected going forward due to a recent reduction in personnel, resulting in improved operating leverage.

In fact, the first-quarter earnings report revealed signs of effective cost-cutting initiatives across multiple segments. Adjusted EBITDA, for example, in Consulting improved by a double-digit percentage despite seeing its revenues fall by 22% in the quarter. Furthermore, the two segments that grew their revenues in the quarter (Europe & Asia Pac, along with Outsourced Services) saw their adjusted EBITDA outperform revenue growth, demonstrating once more that RGP's transformation initiatives continue to gain traction.

Encouraging Q2 Guidance

Furthermore, guidance for Q2 was encouraging (pointing to sustained sales pipeline strength), although the CFO did touch on the minimal ramifications of the US shutdown. Furthermore, RGP continues to make significant inroads on its cost structure, with both gross margin & SG&A expense guidance for Q2 aligning with trends evident in the first quarter earnings report.

On the gross margin front, we also expect similar trends to the first quarter with an outlook range of 38% to 39%, with Thanksgiving adding one additional holiday in the U.S. compared to Q1. Second quarter run rate SG&A expense is expected to be in a range of $43 million to $45 million, reflecting the benefit from our cost reduction efforts. Non-run rate and noncash expenses will be around $5 million, consisting primarily of noncash stock compensation and approximately $2 million of restructuring expense associated with the reduction in force.

Long-Term Chart Demonstrates Shares May Finally Be Zoning In On A Bottom

On the 20-year chart, we can see that RGP stock has experienced carnage over the past 3+ years, with shares falling from well over $20 a share to a mere $5+ a share at present. Such a downswing in the stock, however, can present an opportunity, especially if RGP's current profitability trends can continue in earnest.

As we see below, the long-term MACD looks poised to deliver a bullish signal and will cross over when the rising histogram eventually passes over into positive territory. Long-term MACD bullish signals are potent tools for long-term investors as they play a dual role (momentum & trend) and are usually trustworthy (especially when dealing with an ultra-oversold MACD readout, which is what we have at present in RGP). The one disclaimer, though, for investors is that we do not have a matching bullish divergence in the RSI indicator, which means consolidation may take place for longer than bulls may envisage here.

Valuation Will Attract Investors On Any Meaningful Upmove

Given that the company pays an above-average dividend yield of 5.32%, where there is an ample amount of cash ($77.5 million) on the balance sheet to supplement the payout if indeed cash flow doesn't cover it in any individual quarter, value investors will definitely be attracted to RGP from a long-term perspective. RGP's P/B and P/S multiples are well below historic multiples, the company holds no debt, and, as mentioned, the company's profitability trends are slowly turning for the better.

From a long-term standpoint, however, we would prefer to see the trends we witnessed in Q1 prove themselves over some quarters. If this meant giving up some upside, so be it, but I would opt for seeing some bullish action concerning the stock's longer-term moving averages, for example.

Being Nimble Or Flexible Is A Double-Edged Sword

The CEO's comments on the Q1 earnings call were very much focused on the nimbleness of the firm and how this can be used to RGP's advantage against the competition. However, with the company's market cap having deteriorated to a mere $165 million, a company being 'nimble' with respect to its operations can act as a double-edged sword, especially if market conditions were to change on a dime. We acknowledge that RGP's ongoing transformation through the likes of its Countsy offering, better-trained consultants, and ability to provide value quickly to its customers without undue overhead can be a formula to build its sales pipeline and turn over projects at a faster clip. When a company, however, can win projects faster than before, it can also lose them, which is why we would like to see a successful string of quarterly results before deploying capital on the long side.

Conclusion

Therefore, to sum up, we are reiterating our 'Hold' rating in Resources Connection, Inc., although profitability trends are beginning to change for the better. Let's see now if it can string a few successful quarters together. We look forward to continued coverage.

data.title: CEE: Low PE For Overlooked But Developed Markets In Europe
data.description:
The Central And Eastern Europe Fund, Inc. (NYSE:CEE) is quite interesting. While the Polish markets are quite available to the retail investing public, Hungarian markets less so, and these are major exposures for the CEE fund.

With significant exposure to financials, a big consideration for this fund are the economic outlooks for Hungary and Poland, as well as the outlook for their currencies, neither of which use the EUR. We have concerns about the forint given the direction in manufacturing, and also the still quite high inflation. The Zloty has been doing very well on the increasing prominence of Poland in the European picture thanks to significant economic growth, and greater debt and government spending from a low base. We think that with their overcapitalised government balance sheet, Poland could become a significantly more important player in the European context. The Zloty should be alright. We are more concerned about the forint. But on balance, the fund's portfolio has a low average PE afforded by these overlooked markets, and particularly with the Polish skew we'd be pretty happy with this fund as a way to approach the region's markets. However, net expense ratios are 1.48% - this is actively managed, in a bit of an exotic market so they're making you pay.

CEE Breakdown

The CEE has 64 holdings. The vast majority are in Polish and Hungarian markets, the former being a particularly strong performer since the beginning of 2025. In terms of financial exposures, 38% is just in financials, with exposures in Hungarian and Polish banks being common in this ETF. Otherwise, consumer staples at 14% and energy at 13%.

The financial exposure is going to be highly indexed to these local currencies, though some banks like OTP Bank (OTCPK:OTPGF) have a lot of exposure to other Balkan and Eastern European countries as well - but still around 32% of revenue coming in forint from Hungary. The Polish banks are especially indexed to their own local currency, but also the insurance companies. For many of the consumer staple exposures, the local markets dominate the geographic mix so the FX considerations are important. The economic conditions of Poland and Hungary will also matter for these financial exposures as well as the consumer discretionary exposures at 9% and industrial exposures at 8%.

In the case of Hungary, the forint is under a deal of incremental threat from the fact that manufacturing is under pressure in the country, and inflation is quite high driven by labour market dynamics - namely a limited reserve of labour. Interest rates are some of the highest they are in Europe in Hungary on account of the Hungarian central bank policy making, where the cutting of rates hasn't proceeded beyond the halfway point of the rate rise beginning in 2021. The pressure on the CB to cut rates as the Hungarian elections approach in 2026 will likely be mounting, even though this isn't supposed to influence their decision. The cuts in interest rates would likely hit the forint meaningfully as a more marginal European currency, should cuts occur. With wage-price spiral risks in Hungary, due to the important labour supply component to their inflation dynamics, we feel that it is quite likely that rate cuts occur to address both the manufacturing and economic pressures, but more to the explicit mandate the perpetuating factors for the inflation rate. Rate cuts are also not particularly good for financials' business models either, both in insurance and banking.

Poland is a more positive story. The low debt to GDP ratio (admittedly Hungary also has relatively little debt) means that there are plenty of levers for them to pull in order to drive further growth. They are doing quite a lot of bond auctioning lately in order to fund defense, but with Polish economic growth already significant, and government spending on the rise helping drive growth, we don't see a reason why they couldn't continue quite a bit further - purely from the perspective of the relative productiveness of their economy and the low rates of debt. Government spending is quite a lot of Polish GDP, so the emergence of the Zloty as a strong performing and lately quite reliable emerging country currency is important, on top of the increased prominence of the country on the European stage. We don't really see the need for rate cuts, though debt auctioning could put pressure on the Zloty as the supply of Zloty-based instruments rises against demand. But we feel the Zloty is more defended.

Bottom Line

The PE of the portfolio is around 8.58x offering a significant earnings yield of around 12%. While Hungary is under a bit of pressure, either in terms of somewhat muted economic activity or if rate cuts come in the hit to the forint that will affect the Hungarian stocks' forint-based income, Poland is a much more positive economic story, where the government is overcapitalised, has a lot of debt capacity to drive economic growth, and where we don't think debt auctioning will particularly sour the view on the Zloty, particularly with a strong economy precluding the need for significant rate cuts. For the financial and discretionary exposures, a good economic picture in Poland which is the bulk of CEE exposure puts the fund in a decent enough position at this low PE, offering a lot of earnings yield for these overlooked yet still developed economic markets. As for the energy and oil exposure, the return of UAE and Saudi capacity as OPEC+ phases out supply cuts is a bit of a pressure - though OPEC+ is shooting low to drag out the supply cuts as much as possible, but only because of the genuine concerns around demand. At least energy is the only particularly global exposure at around 13% of the portfolio. In all, we like CEE.

data.title: We Disagree With Mr. Market On Occidental Petroleum
data.description:
Occidental Petroleum Corporation (NYSE: NYSE:OXY) is a major petrochemical firm, with an almost $45 billion market capitalization. That's after the major hit the company's stock took last week when it announced a $9.7 billion acquisition of its OxyChem business by Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (BRK.B).

While we think the company made a mistake by not redeeming Berkshire Hathaway's preferred equity, as we'll see throughout this article, it's an intelligent decision at Occidental Petroleum's current valuation.

Occidental Petroleum Strategic Move

Occidental Petroleum is selling OxyChem to Berkshire Hathaway for $9.7 billion in cash.

Notably, this transaction does not replace the similar amount of preferred equity that Berkshire Hathaway holds, that comes in at a lofty 8% interest rate. The transaction is expected to close by the end of the year, with $6.5 billion going to immediate debt repayment and $1.5 billion in after-tax proceeds to the balance sheet.

The tax cost here is another impact, a $1.7 billion bill that the company is eating to repay debt. The company will achieve its <$15 billion debt target, enabling it to accelerate returns and share repurchases, which we think is valuable at its current valuation.

OxyChem is impacted by a variety of chemical prices, and new volumes are growing substantially. The business owns 23 facilities across the world, with expected 2025E pre-tax income at roughly $850 million. The company's tax rate puts that at roughly $600 million post-tax. That means the company sold it for ~15x earnings, not a bad rate for the industry.

That's especially true because while 2022 prices were strong, the 2010s' prices were much lower.

Occidental Petroleum Differentiated Portfolio

Occidental Petroleum might be losing its expensive chem business, but the company has an impressive differentiated portfolio.

The company plans to focus on its oil and gas business, with its vast resource position. Occidental Petroleum is continuing to improve its cost efficiencies and progress in technology recovery. The company expects to achieve >$2 billion in annualized cost savings from this portfolio over a few years, and the company expects this optimization will lead to improved FCF.

Occidental Petroleum has prime positions in some of the lowest-cost basins, such as the Delaware and Midland Basins, with new developments. The company is also working on creating new opportunities in its existing operating basin. Improvement in technologies here will enable the company to continue saving on costs and improve its returns.

The company is also improving its conventional portfolio, which will also support improved returns.

Occidental Petroleum Return Of Capital

The net result of the day is that, combined with share price weakness, Occidental Petroleum will be able to improve capital returns.

The company will hit its post-CrownRock debt target of <$15 billion, which will enable it to launch a multi-year share repurchase program. Occidental Petroleum expects to retire the remaining debt at maturity with no need to purchase future debt. Occidental Petroleum expects to start preferred equity redemption in August 2029.

That's $800 million in annualized cash savings, and it will be preceded by cash. The company's 2.1% dividend is modest but is affordable and will keep growing. The company has $1 billion in quarterly FCF, which puts it at a 9% FCF yield. 2026/2027 FCF growth will more than make up for OxyChem, and enable continued strength in returns.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is continued weakness in oil prices. WTI is sitting at just under $63/barrel. Below $60/barrel WTI, Occidental Petroleum will only generate single-digit returns at its current price, even with an impressive asset base. That risk, especially with an admin focused on lower prices, and a downturn, is a major risk.

Conclusion

Occidental Petroleum has been punished by the market for its $9.7 billion sale of OxyChem to Berkshire Hathaway. At the same time, the company did that sale without getting back its preferred equity from Berkshire Hathaway, preferred equity that's costing it $800 million/year. Instead, the company is paying taxes and paying off debt.

Despite all that, Occidental Petroleum has an impressive portfolio of assets, and it's working to lower its breakeven and increase FCF. With its reduced debt, the company can focus on share repurchases with its cash and its strong dividend, making it a valuable investment proposition. Let us know your thoughts in the comments below.

data.title: Precious Metals Lead Today, Diversification Wins Tomorrow
data.description:
Precious metals led Q3 commodity gains, with gold and silver rallying on central bank buying and safe-haven demand. Broader diversification across sectors positions commodities for durable, long-term performance.

Steady Gains, Diverging Drivers

Commodity index products delivered steady gains in the third quarter, extending what has been a positive year for the asset class. The UBS CM Commodity Index (CMCITR) advanced 2.82%, while the Bloomberg Commodity Index (BCOM) gained 3.65%. CMCITR underperformed BCOM both quarter-to-date and year-to-date. The difference comes down to precious metals, which carry a larger weight in BCOM’s structure than in CMCITR’s.

Precious Metals Take Center Stage

Gold and silver have been the standout performers of 2025. In Q3, gold rose 16% and is now up nearly 47% year-to-date. Silver gained 28% in the quarter and an extraordinary 64% year-to-date. Precious metals are therefore the best-performing asset class in the world this year. BCOM, with its heavier allocation to gold and silver, captured all of its Q3 gains from this sector. CMCITR, by contrast, generated more balanced gains across the commodity complex, apart from agriculture, underscoring its diversified design.

BCOM’s YTD gains are attributed to heavier allocation to the precious metals sector

Comparative Index Sector Weights

Estimated Q3 2025 Contribution to Return by Sector

Why Gold and Silver Are Rallying

Several themes have fueled gold and silver’s rally. Central banks continue to diversify away from U.S. dollar assets, steadily increasing their gold holdings. Gold has also benefited from its role as the world’s ultimate safety asset amid geopolitical risks and fiscal concerns. More recently, Western investors have joined the trend, using gold to hedge against market volatility and U.S. policy uncertainty. Silver has moved in tandem, boosted by safe-haven demand but also by its industrial role in electrification, technology, and renewable energy. This dual demand has amplified silver’s rise, sending it to multi-year highs.

CMCITR’s Strength Lies in Diversification

While BCOM’s concentration in precious metals has been a clear advantage this year, CMCITR’s broader allocation is built for resilience. In the third quarter, its gains were more evenly spread across energy and industrial metals. That balance highlights the benefit of diversification when leadership shifts across sectors.

Positioning for the Future: Balance Provides Clear Opportunities

Looking ahead, CMCITR’s emphasis on industrial metals provides a structural advantage. The global shift to cleaner energy, the buildout of electric power infrastructure, and the rapid growth of AI computing all rely heavily on copper, aluminum, nickel, and other critical inputs. These forces are only beginning to accelerate and are likely to drive strong demand in the years ahead. CMCITR’s diversified exposure positions it to capture these opportunities, even if precious metals cool from their extraordinary run. Gold may be golden today, but over full cycles, balance wins. With its broader sector allocation and greater weight in industrial metals, CMCITR is well-positioned to deliver durable outperformance over the long term.

Disclosures

This is not an offer to buy or sell, or a recommendation to buy or sell any of the securities, financial instruments or digital assets mentioned herein. The information presented does not involve the rendering of personalized investment, financial, legal, tax advice, or any call to action. Certain statements contained herein may constitute projections, forecasts and other forward-looking statements, which do not reflect actual results, are for illustrative purposes only, are valid as of the date of this communication, and are subject to change without notice. Actual future performance of any assets or industries mentioned are unknown. Information provided by third party sources are believed to be reliable and have not been independently verified for accuracy or completeness and cannot be guaranteed. VanEck does not guarantee the accuracy of third-party data. The information herein represents the opinion of the author(s), but not necessarily those of VanEck or its other employees.

All indices are unmanaged and include the reinvestment of all dividends, but do not reflect the payment of transaction costs, advisory fees or expenses that are associated with an investment in the Fund. An index’s performance is not illustrative of the Fund’s performance. Indices are not securities in which investments can be made. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

The UBS Bloomberg Constant Maturity Commodity Index (CMCITR) is a total return rules-based composite benchmark index diversified across 29 commodity components from within five sectors, specifically energy, precious metals, industrial metals, agricultural and livestock.

Bloomberg Commodity Index (BCOM) provides broad-based exposure to commodities, and no single commodity or commodity sector dominates the index. Rather than being driven by micro-economic events affecting one commodity market or sector, the diversified commodity exposure of BCOM potentially reduces volatility in comparison with non-diversified commodity investments.

UBS AG AND ITS AFFILIATES (“UBS”) DO NOT SPONSOR, ENDORSE, SELL, OR PROMOTE CM COMMODITY INDEX FUND (THE “PRODUCT”). A DECISION TO INVEST IN THE PRODUCT SHOULD NOT BE MADE IN RELIANCE ON ANY OF THE STATEMENTS SET FORTH IN THIS WEBSITE. PROSPECTIVE INVESTORS ARE ADVISED TO MAKE AN INVESTMENT IN THE PRODUCT ONLY AFTER CAREFULLY CONSIDERING THE RISKS ASSOCIATED WITH INVESTING IN THE PRODUCT, AS DETAILED IN THE PROSPECTUS THAT IS PREPARED BY OR ON BEHALF OF VANECK (“LICENSEE”), THE ISSUER OF THE PRODUCT. UBS HAS LICENSED CERTAIN UBS MARKS AND OTHER DATA TO LICENSEE FOR USE IN CONNECTION WITH THE PRODUCT AND THE BRANDING OF THE PRODUCT, BUT UBS IS NOT INVOLVED IN THE CALCULATION OF THE PRODUCT, THE CONSTRUCTION OF THE PRODUCT’S METHODOLOGY OR THE CREATION OF THE PRODUCT, NOR IS UBS INVOLVED IN THE SALE OR OFFERING OF THE PRODUCT, AND UBS DOES NOT MAKE ANY REPRESENTATIONS OR WARRANTIES WITH RESPECT TO THE PRODUCT AND DISCLAIMS ANY LIABILITY FOR ANY INACCURACY, ERROR OR DELAY IN, OR OMISSION OF THE DATA.

Investments in commodities can be very volatile and direct investment in these markets can be very risky, especially for inexperienced investors.

VanEck CM Commodity Index Fund: You can lose money by investing in the Fund. Any investment in the Fund should be part of an overall investment program, not a complete program. Commodities are assets that have tangible properties, such as oil, metals, and agriculture. Commodities and commodity-linked derivatives may be affected by overall market movements and other factors that affect the value of a particular industry or commodity, such as weather, disease, embargoes or political or regulatory developments. The value of a commodity-linked derivative is generally based on price movements of a commodity, a commodity futures contract, a commodity index or other economic variables based on the commodity markets. Derivatives use leverage, which may exaggerate a loss. An investment in the Fund may be subject to risks which include, but are not limited to, risks related to active management, commodities and commodity-linked derivatives, commodity regulatory, credit, derivatives counterparty, derivatives, government-related bond, index tracking, industry concentration, investments in money market funds, interest rate, LIBOR replacement, market, operational, and subsidiary investment risk, all of which may adversely affect the Fund. The use of commodity-linked derivatives such as swaps, commodity-linked structured notes and futures entails substantial risks, including risk of loss of a significant portion of their principal value, lack of a secondary market, increased volatility, correlation, liquidity, interest-rate, valuation and tax risks. Gains and losses from speculative positions in derivatives may be much greater than the derivative’s cost. At any time, the risk of loss of any individual security held by the Fund could be significantly higher than 50% of the security’s value. Investment in commodity markets may not be suitable for all investors. The Fund’s investment in commodity-linked derivative instruments may subject the Fund to greater volatility than investment in traditional securities.

VanEck CMCI Commodity Strategy ETF: An investment in the Fund may be subject to risks which include, among others, risks related to investing in the agricultural commodity sector, commodities and commodity-linked instruments, commodities and commodity-linked instruments tax, derivatives counterparty, energy commodity sector, metals commodity sector, U.S. treasury bills, Subsidiary investment, commodity regulatory and tax risks with respect to investments in the Subsidiary, gap, cash transactions, credit, debt securities, interest rate, derivatives, commodity index tracking, repurchase agreements, regulatory, market, operational, authorized participant concentration, new fund, no guarantee of active trading market, trading issues, fund shares trading, premium/discount risk and liquidity of fund shares, non-diversified, and commodity index-related concentration risks, all of which may adversely affect the Fund. The use of commodity-linked derivatives such as swaps, commodity-linked structured notes and futures entails substantial risks, including risk of loss of a significant portion of their principal value, lack of a secondary market, increased volatility, correlation, liquidity, interest-rate, valuation and tax risks. Investment in commodity markets may not be suitable for all investors. The Fund’s investment in commodity-linked derivative instruments may subject the Fund to greater volatility than investment in traditional securities. The level of derivatives counterparty risk may be heightened due to the Fund currently only having a single counterparty available with which to enter into swap contracts on the Index.

Investing involves substantial risk and high volatility, including possible loss of principal. An investor should consider the investment objective, risks, charges and expenses of a Fund carefully before investing. To obtain a prospectus and summary prospectus, which contain this and other information, call 800.826.2333 or visit vaneck.com. Please read the prospectus and summary prospectus carefully before investing.

data.title: Occidental Petroleum: My Take On The OxyChem Deal
data.description:
At first blush on the rumors, I thought the market would warmly accept a sale of OxyChem. Yes, the implied multiple was rather low: 6.1x 2024 EBITDA, a bit lower for 2025. Many investment banks had been building in higher valuations for this business on a sum-of-the-parts basis, but I don't believe that was the major reason that this deal was panned as a bad one - at least on its own. Those reasons, we'll get into.

To start, I don't think Occidental (NYSE:OXY) really got credit for the chemicals portfolio. Only roughly 15-20% of earnings came from the chemicals division, and while I agree that the chemicals division deserves a higher multiple, I don't think many investors looking at Occidental cared much for it being a part of the corporation. Shedding it transitions the company to being much closer to being a pure play exploration and production ("E&P") company, which will allow it to be valued much better compared to its peers, which remain large independents like EOG Resources (EOG) or Diamondback Energy (FANG) rather than the majors that do have more diverse earnings streams (like Exxon Mobil (XOM)).

The $6,500mm in "free funds" from this deal are set to be allocated towards debt reduction (some cash will be retained, some lost to taxes) which does rapidly accelerate the company reaching its $15,000mm debt target. Many expected that this would not be reached for six to eight quarters, with the company then looking to target the Berkshire owned preferreds ($8,500mm worth) before the end of the decade. These are big numbers, and shareholder returns have been understandably constrained given the debt situation at Occidental. Dividends and share repurchases of any material size have been repressed, and outside of the "Follow Warren Buffett" crowd, most institutional investors (and myself) have been far more interested in large cap E&P with greater short term return potential. On paper, shareholders get to the point that they paid bigger bucks faster after this deal.

Negative Sides Of This Deal

Once final details of the sale were released though, the market responded with a sell down. Number one, and the most obvious, was the speculation that the sale would be made for $10,000mm. Thus, the sale came in lower than expected, but the $300mm is small potatoes compared to the more than $3,000mm in equity value that was wiped out during trading. There are two, larger pieces at work here on the negative reaction.

Number one is the environmental liabilities. Occidental does not break out environmental liabilities between its operational segment in its 10-K, but pretty normal for operations of this size to have billions in gross liabilities related to winding down operations. And what is carried on the balance sheet could end up being understated. Backing into a net present value for those obligations depends on a lot of assumptions, but most estimates I've seen peg the figure at between $700-$1,100mm in net present value ("NPV") costs associated with asset retirement. This sale importantly does not transfer these costs to Berkshire Hathaway; when the time comes for cleanup, Occidental is on the hook. That's not necessarily unusual, but it also really makes the EBITDA multiple on the sale questionable. Chemical plants, upstream oil and gas, refineries, and businesses like these carry lower EBITDA multiples in the market at least in part because of these future liabilities. Berkshire is getting OxyChem free of any costs related to some of its legacy issues (ancient chlor-alkali facilities, chromium waste pits, the Love Canal site) for a pretty great deal.

Also missing was a preferred for OxyChem swap. In theory, Occidental could transfer OxyChem shares to Berkshire in exchange for the Preferreds they hold, which under Section 351/Section 368 would be a non-recognition event. Because no cash consideration changes hands, this helps avoid triggering capital gains or income taxes, which is an issue with OxyChem; the tax man is getting a lot of money in an all-cash deal.

Investors really wanted to see this. A swap would have eliminated a relatively expensive liability (8.0% coupon) in a tax-advantaged manner; one the company was already planning on tackling late in this decade. It looks like Berkshire Hathaway doesn't want to give up its income stream from its preferreds any time soon, and is going to make the company tackle it organically. It knows that the leverage target comes first, so any cash Occidental gets from this deal isn't going to be buying the preferreds. It's shrewd, but it's business.

With there being $1,700mm in "tax leakage" since any preferred swap has now been scuttled, investors are penalizing Occidental for not being able to come to an agreement up to and including that amount.

Takeaways

If you add up these three factors (deal coming in lower, environmental liability obligation, tax leakage) you do roughly get the number that the stock fell on the day of the announcement. In summary, the price for OxyChem was low on its own, but it's made worse by it being an all cash deal and there being no sharing of environmental liabilities. I think the market could have let these things go with a higher price, but it looks like Berkshire got both a rock bottom price and everything it wanted from a transaction setup as well.

data.title: Western Alliance: Don't Worry About First Brands
data.description:
Shares of Western Alliance Bancorporation (NYSE:WAL) dropped off more than 4% on Wednesday after reports circulated that the regional bank was financially exposed to the recent collapse of auto-parts marker First Brands Group. Western Alliance is said to have exposure to a portfolio of receivables that are linked to the company, causing selling pressure. I believe that investor concerns about this exposure are overblown given recent statements made by investment bank Jefferies, and the drop may constitute a buying opportunity for investors that have a favorable long-term view on the bank.

Previous rating

I originally bought Western Alliance Bancorporation as a contrarian bet during the regional banking meltdown after the collapse of Silicon Valley Bank in 2023. Most recently, after the lender reported solid second quarter earnings amid strong momentum in net interest income, I rated shares of Western Alliance a Buy: Solid Growth, Strong NII Gives Upside Potential. I believe current concerns about the bank's exposure to First Brands Group's receivables are exaggerated and the fallout, even in the worst-case scenario, is most likely contained.

First Brands Group situation

First Brands Group is an auto-parts supplier that filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection last month. Regional bank Western Alliance is said to have financial exposure to this company via a leveraged credit facility with Point Bonita Capital, which has renewed attention lately.

Point Bonita Capital is a trade finance platform that is managed by a division of Leucadia Asset Management which itself is part of investment bank Jefferies. Companies like First Brands Group can sell their receivables to third-party investors (a practice called "factoring") in order to shorten their cash conversion cycles and improve their liquidity positions.

Receivables that are owed by First Brands have been sold to third-party funds at a discount in exchange for immediate payment. Now that First Brands Group has fallen into bankruptcy, investors are concerned that a potential non-payment of receivables by the firm’s counterparties may negatively affect Western Alliances’ investment in the credit facility.

Point Bonita Capital’s portfolio, according to a recent disclosure by investment bank Jefferies, has about $715 million invested in receivables that are to be paid by companies like Walmart (WMT) and AutoZone (AZO):

Point Bonita Capital, a division of Leucadia Asset Management (“LAM”), manages on behalf of third-party institutional and other investors an approximately $3 billion portfolio of trade-finance assets, which is supported by total invested equity of $1.9 billion, of which $113 million, or 5.9%, is owned by LAM… Point Bonita portfolio has approximately $715 million invested in receivables.

Further, Jefferies provided further clarity on the situation on Wednesday (via Seeking Alpha):

Presently, we have no reason to believe the events from the First Brands bankruptcy create a material risk of loss in connection with the credit at this time. LAM is independently pursuing their own rights and remedies under their governing credit documents separately and apart from their obligations to Western Alliance

The exact economic exposure of Western Alliance to the receivables portfolio mentioned above is not specified, but it is unlikely that the current problems are going to have a significant impact on the bank’s earnings, in my opinion.

Even if we were to assume that the full $715M investment was provided by Western Alliance (which is highly unlikely), this amount represents only about 3.3% of the regional bank’s total securities and total cash position, and approximately 0.8% of its total assets. In other words, even in the extremely unlikely case that Western Alliance provided 100% of the investment in the leveraged credit facility with Point Bonita Capital, the bank’s operations would likely continue as normal and a potential earnings impact would not be very significant.

Further, any losses sustained here would likely be non-recurring only. In my opinion, the recent news about First Brands Group is more a distraction than anything else: Western Alliance is a highly profitable regional banking franchise and should be able to report strong earnings on October 21, 2025.

Estimates imply 21% Y/Y EPS growth in Q3

While investors clearly disliked Wednesday's news about Western Alliance’s exposure to First Brands Group-related receivables, the regional bank is profitable and it should remain so going forward, unless it were to experience a considerable deterioration in asset quality... which would likely only happen in case of a U.S. recession.

The earnings estimate trend so far has been slightly negative... mainly because the regional bank faces prospects for declining net interest income in a falling-rate world. In the last 90 days, analysts have revised their Q3 EPS estimates upwards 0 times which compares to 14 downward revisions. Analysts currently expect $2.07 in adjusted Q3 EPS for Western Alliance which still implies a respectable year-over-year growth rate of ~21%.

WAL’s valuation

Shares of Western Alliance Bancorporation are trading at a premium to book value: currently, investors pay a 33% mark-up to BV, but this does not make the regional bank expensive.

As opposed to the April-June 2023 period, which is when regional banks traded at massive discounts to book value following the collapse of Silicon Valley Bank, most regional banks now trade at premiums to book value.

Western Alliance's shares are still relatively cheap in a historical context: in the last five years, the regional lender achieved, on average, a price-to-book ratio of 1.63X. The industry group average P/B -- which is made up of the price-to-book ratios of U.S. Bancorp (USB), KeyCorp (KEY) and Fifth Third Bancorp (FITB) -- is 1.33X, meaning shares of WAL are currently trading at about the industry group average.

If shares were to revalue to the longer term P/B ratio of 1.63X, shares of Western Alliance could have a fair value in the neighborhood of $101 per-share. This price target could be achieved if the regional lender continues to grow its book value and maintains high bank profitability. My price target, considering a closing price of $82.35 per-share on Wednesday, implies 22% upside revaluation potential.

Risks with WAL

What I consider more of a risk to Western Alliance's financial performance than the leveraged facility provided to Point Bonita Capital is a potential acceleration of the Federal Reserve's rate cut trajectory. The Federal Reserve cut interest rates 25 basis points in September to a new range of 4.00% to 4.25%, which is generally a negative for banks that lend out their money at higher interest rates to borrowers. Further, a deterioration in asset quality remains a long term risk factor for investors, especially if the U.S. economy were to fall into a recession.

Final thoughts

Western Alliance has some exposure to First Brands Group, a bankrupt auto-parts supplier (although no party has quantified this exposure just yet), but it is unlikely that WAL faces a serious financial risk here, in my opinion.

Receivables portfolios tend to be diversified, and Jefferies itself said that it doesn’t see a material risk for investors here. As such, I believe the drop may actually constitute a buying opportunity considering that Western Alliance should be set for a reasonably strong earnings report for the third-quarter.

The bank's consensus EPS estimates indicate 21% year-over-year growth for Q3 and if the regional bank continues to grow its book value, I believe Western Alliance has a solid chance to revalue to my fair value estimate of $101/share.
